data.architecture-overview#Architecture Overv: Loclean is built on a hybrid architecture that combines the speed of regex with the intelligence of local LLMs, all while maintaining privacy and determinism.
data.1-hybrid-router#1. Hybrid Router:
Loclean uses a hybrid approach that intelligently routes between regex and LLM processing:
Benefits:
Fast regex for simple patterns
LLM for complex, ambiguous cases
Automatic fallback and retry
data.2-gbnf-grammar-generation#2. GBNF Gramma:
Loclean automatically converts Pydantic schemas to GBNF (Grammar-Based Neural Format) grammars:
from pydantic import BaseModel
class Product(BaseModel):
    name: str
    price: int
    color: str
# Loclean automatically generates GBNF grammar:
# - Forces LLM to output valid JSON
# - Ensures schema compliance
# - Prevents hallucinations
How it works:
Parse Pydantic schema
Convert to JSON Schema
Generate GBNF grammar rules
Constrain LLM output to valid structure
data.3-inference-engine#3. Inference Engine:
The inference engine manages model loading, caching, and execution:
Features:
Automatic model downloading
Persistent result caching
Retry logic with prompt adjustment
Batch processing optimization
data.4-backend-agnostic-design#4. Backend Agn:
Built on Narwhals, Loclean supports multiple DataFrame backends transparently:
# Works with any Narwhals-compatible backend
import pandas as pd
import polars as pl
# Pandas
df_pd = pd.DataFrame({"data": ["5kg", "3kg"]})
result_pd = loclean.clean(df_pd, target_col="data")
# Returns: pandas.DataFrame
# Polars
df_pl = pl.DataFrame({"data": ["5kg", "3kg"]})
result_pl = loclean.clean(df_pl, target_col="data")
# Returns: polars.DataFrame
Benefits:
Zero-copy operations
Preserves lazy evaluation (Polars)
No vendor lock-in
Seamless backend switching

data.overview#Overview:
The clean() function is Loclean's core data cleaning API. It uses semantic extraction to clean and normalize messy data in DataFrame columns, making it perfect for handling inconsistent formats, units, and representations.
See also: Quick Start | Structured Extraction | API Reference
data.basic-usage#Basic Usage:
import loclean
import polars as pl
# Create a DataFrame with messy data
df = pl.DataFrame({
    "weight": ["5kg", "3.5 kg", "5000g", "2.2kg"]
})
# Clean the weight column
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="weight",
    instruction="Extract the numeric value and unit as-is."
)
# Result includes new columns:
# - clean_value: The extracted numeric value
# - clean_unit: The extracted unit
# - clean_reasoning: LLM's reasoning for the extraction
print(result)
Output:

The clean() function adds three new columns to your DataFrame:
clean_value: The extracted numeric value (as float)
clean_unit: The extracted unit (as string)
clean_reasoning: The LLM's reasoning process (as string)

data.batch-processing#Batch Processing:
For large datasets, clean() automatically processes data in batches:
# Process 100 items per batch (default: 50)
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="weight",
    instruction="Extract weight value and unit",
    batch_size=100
)
data.parallel-processing#Parallel Processing:
Enable parallel processing for faster execution on large datasets:
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="weight",
    instruction="Extract weight value and unit",
    parallel=True,  # Enable parallel processing
    max_workers=4   # Use 4 worker threads
)
data.model-selection#Model Selection:
You can specify a different model for cleaning:
# Use a faster model for simple extractions
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="weight",
    instruction="Extract weight value and unit",
    model_name="tinyllama"  # Faster, smaller model
)
data.advanced-configuration#Advanced Configur:
For advanced use cases, you can configure the underlying engine:
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="weight",
    instruction="Extract weight value and unit",
    n_ctx=8192,        # Larger context window
    n_gpu_layers=10,   # Use GPU acceleration
    cache_dir="/custom/cache/path"
)
data.best-practices#Best Practices:
Start with simple instructions: Begin with basic instructions and refine as needed
Use appropriate batch sizes: Larger batches = faster but more memory usage
Enable parallel for large datasets: Use parallel=True for datasets with >1000 rows
Choose the right model: Use tinyllama for speed, phi-3-mini for balance, larger models for complex extractions
Cache results: Results are automatically cached to avoid redundant processing

data.how-it-works#How It Works:
The extract() function ensures 100% compliance with your Pydantic schema through:
Dynamic GBNF Grammar Generation: Automatically converts Pydantic schemas to GBNF grammars
JSON Repair: Automatically fixes malformed JSON output from LLMs
Retry Logic: Retries with adjusted prompts when validation fails

Running Polars? We keep it lazy.

data.optional-dependencies#Optional Dependenc:
The basic installation includes local inference support (via llama-cpp-python). Loclean uses Narwhals for backend-agnostic DataFrame operations, so if you already have Pandas, Polars, or PyArrow installed, the basic installation is sufficient.Install DataFrame libraries (if not already present):If you don't have any DataFrame library installed, or want to ensure you have all supported backends:
pip install loclean[data]
This installs: pandas>=2.3.3, polars>=0.20.0, pyarrow>=22.0.0For Cloud API support (OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini):Cloud API support is planned for future releases. Currently, only local inference is available:
pip install loclean[cloud]
Install all optional dependencies:
pip install loclean[all]
This installs both loclean[data] and loclean[cloud]. Useful for production environments where you want all features available.
Note for developers: If you're contributing to Loclean, use the Development Installation section below (git clone + uv sync --dev), not loclean[all].

data.overview#Overview: Loclean's structured extraction feature allows you to extract complex, structured data from unstructured text with 100% schema compliance using Pydantic models and GBNF grammars.
data.how-it-works#How It Works:
Define Your Schema: Create a Pydantic model that represents the data structure you want to extract
Provide Input: Pass unstructured text or a DataFrame column
Automatic Extraction: Loclean uses LLM with GBNF grammar to extract data matching your schema
Guaranteed Compliance: If the output doesn't match your schema, it retries automatically
data.example#Example:
from pydantic import BaseModel
import loclean
class Product(BaseModel):
    name: str
    price: int
    color: str
# Extract from text
item = loclean.extract("Selling red t-shirt for 50k", schema=Product)
print(item.name)  # "t-shirt"
print(item.price)  # 50000
print(item.color)  # "red"
Output:

data.nested-schemas#Nested Schemas:
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import List, Optional
class Address(BaseModel):
    street: str
    city: str
    state: str
    zip_code: str
class Person(BaseModel):
    name: str
    age: int
    email: str
    address: Address
    phone_numbers: List[str]
    notes: Optional[str] = None
text = """
John Doe, age 35, email: john@example.com
Lives at 123 Main St, New York, NY 10001
Phones: 555-1234, 555-5678
Notes: Preferred contact method is email
"""
person = loclean.extract(text, schema=Person)
print(person.name)              # "John Doe"
print(person.address.city)      # "New York"
print(person.phone_numbers)     # ["555-1234", "555-5678"]
Output:
John Doe
New York
['555-1234', '555-5678']
data.union-types#Union Types:
from pydantic import BaseModel
from typing import Union
class EmailContact(BaseModel):
    type: str = "email"
    address: str
class PhoneContact(BaseModel):
    type: str = "phone"
    number: str
Contact = Union[EmailContact, PhoneContact]
class Customer(BaseModel):
    name: str
    contact: Contact
# LLM will extract the appropriate type
customer = loclean.extract(
    "Customer: John Doe, email: john@example.com",
    schema=Customer
)
data.error-handling#Error Handling:
Loclean automatically retries failed extractions:
# Automatic retry on validation failure

    text,
    schema=ComplexSchema,
    max_retries=3  # Default: 3 retries
)
If all retries fail, a ValidationError is raised with details about what went wrong.

data.overview#Overview: Loclean is designed for performance, but following these best practices will help you get the most out of it, especially when processing large datasets.
data.batch-processing#Batch Processing:
The clean() and extract() functions automatically process data in batches. Adjust batch size based on your data:
# Small batches for complex extractions (more accurate)
result = loclean.extract(
    df,
    schema=ComplexSchema,
    target_col="text",
    batch_size=25  # Smaller batches for complex schemas
)
# Large batches for simple extractions (faster)
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="weight",
    instruction="Extract weight",
    batch_size=100  # Larger batches for simple tasks
)
Recommendations:
Simple extractions: 50-100 items per batch
Complex schemas: 25-50 items per batch
Very large datasets: 100-200 items per batch
data.parallel-processing#Parallel Processing:
Enable parallel processing for datasets with >1000 rows:
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="data",
    instruction="Extract value",
    parallel=True,      # Enable parallel processing
    max_workers=None    # Auto-detect optimal worker count
)
When to use:
вң… Large datasets (more than 1000 rows)
вң… Simple extraction tasks
вң… Multiple CPU cores available
вқҢ Small datasets (less than 100 rows) - overhead not worth it
вқҢ Very complex schemas - may cause memory issues
data.model-selection#Model Selection: Choose the right model for your use case:
data.for-speed#For Speed:
# Use tinyllama for fastest processing
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="data",
    model_name="tinyllama"  # Smallest, fastest model
)
Best for: Simple extractions, large datasets, real-time processing
data.for-balance#For Balance:
# Use phi-3-mini for balanced performance (default)
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="data",
    model_name="phi-3-mini"  # Default, good balance
)
Best for: Most use cases, moderate complexity
data.for-quality#For Quality:
# Use larger models for complex extractions
result = loclean.extract(
    df,
    schema=ComplexSchema,
    model_name="qwen3-4b"  # Higher quality, slower
)
Best for: Complex schemas, high accuracy requirements

By default, cache is stored in ~/.cache/loclean. You can customize:

data.process-in-chunks#Process in Chunks:
For very large datasets, process in chunks:
import polars as pl
# Process 10,000 rows at a time
chunk_size = 10000
results = []
for i in range(0, len(df), chunk_size):
    chunk = df.slice(i, chunk_size)
    result = loclean.clean(chunk, target_col="data")
    results.append(result)
# Combine results
final_result = pl.concat(results)
data.use-lazy-evaluation-polars#Use Lazy Eval:
Polars lazy evaluation is preserved:
import polars as pl
df = pl.scan_csv("large_file.csv")  # Lazy DataFrame
# Clean operation
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="data",
    instruction="Extract value"
)
# Still lazy - no computation yet
filtered = result.filter(pl.col("clean_value") > 100)
# Compute only when needed
final = filtered.collect()

Loclean provides privacy-first data cleaning by scrubbing sensitive PII (Personally Identifiable Information) data locally. No data leaves your machine.
See also: Quick Start | Structured Extraction | API Reference
data.features#Features:
Local Processing: All processing happens on your machine using local LLMs
Hybrid Approach: Combines Regex patterns and LLM-based detection for accuracy
Zero Cost: No cloud API calls, completely free to use
Privacy Guaranteed: Your sensitive data never leaves your infrastructure

data.why-loclean#Why Loclean?: Loclean bridges the gap between Data Engineering and Local AI, designed for production pipelines where privacy and stability are non-negotiable.
data.key-features#Key Features:
Privacy First: Scrub sensitive PII data locally using Regex & LLMs. No data leaves your machine. Zero cloud costs.
Structured Extraction: Extract complex data into Pydantic models with 100% schema compliance using GBNF grammars. No hallucinations.
Local & Fast: Powered by llama-cpp-python and quantized GGUF models. Optimized for CPU with optional GPU acceleration.
Backend Agnostic: Works seamlessly with Pandas, Polars, and PyArrow via Narwhals. Zero-copy, preserves lazy evaluation.
Deterministic Outputs: GBNF grammars and Pydantic V2 force valid, type-safe JSON. If it breaks the schema, it doesn't pass.
Production Ready: Built for data pipelines. Batch processing, parallel execution, SQLite caching, and comprehensive error handling.

import loclean
from pydantic import BaseModel
class Transaction(BaseModel):
    amount: float
    currency: str
    date: str

    "Paid $50.00 to Amazon on 2024-01-15 for Electronics",
    "Received 100 EUR from Client ABC on Jan 20, 2024 - Invoice payment",
    "Withdrawal: $200 cash on 2024-01-18"
]

)

)

data.locleanclean#loclean.clean():
Clean a column in a DataFrame using semantic extraction.
def clean(
    df: IntoFrameT,
    target_col: str,
    instruction: str = "Extract the numeric value and unit as-is.",
    *,
    model_name: Optional[str] = None,
    cache_dir: Optional[Path] = None,
    n_ctx: Optional[int] = None,
    n_gpu_layers: Optional[int] = None,
    batch_size: int = 50,
    parallel: bool = False,
    max_workers: Optional[int] = None,
    **engine_kwargs: Any,
) -> IntoFrameT
data.parameters#Parameters:
df (IntoFrameT): Input DataFrame (pandas, Polars, Modin, cuDF, PyArrow, etc.)
target_col (str): Name of the column to clean
instruction (str): Instruction to guide the LLM extraction. Default: "Extract the numeric value and unit as-is."
model_name (Optional[str]): Model identifier (e.g., 'phi-3-mini', 'tinyllama'). Default: uses configured default
cache_dir (Optional[Path]): Custom directory for caching models. Default: ~/.cache/loclean
n_ctx (Optional[int]): Context window size override
n_gpu_layers (Optional[int]): Number of GPU layers to use (0 = CPU only)
batch_size (int): Number of unique values to process per batch. Default: 50
parallel (bool): Enable parallel processing. Default: False
max_workers (Optional[int]): Maximum worker threads. Default: None (auto-detect)
engine_kwargs (Any): Additional arguments forwarded to LlamaCppEngine
data.returns#Returns:
DataFrame with added columns:
{target_col}_clean_value: Extracted numeric value (float)
{target_col}_clean_unit: Extracted unit (string)
{target_col}_clean_reasoning: LLM reasoning (string)
Return type matches input type (pandas вҶ’ pandas, Polars вҶ’ Polars, etc.)

import loclean

data.locleanextract#loclean.extract():
Extract structured data from unstructured text using Pydantic schemas.
def extract(
    input_data: str | IntoFrameT,
    schema: type[Any],
    instruction: str | None = None,
    *,
    target_col: str | None = None,
    output_type: str = "dict",
    max_retries: int = 3,
    model_name: Optional[str] = None,
    cache_dir: Optional[Path] = None,
    n_ctx: Optional[int] = None,
    n_gpu_layers: Optional[int] = None,
    **engine_kwargs: Any,
) -> Any | IntoFrameT
data.parameters-1#Parameters:
input_data (str | IntoFrameT): Input text or DataFrame
schema (type[Any]): Pydantic model class defining the extraction schema
instruction (Optional[str]): Custom instruction. Default: auto-generated from schema
target_col (Optional[str]): DataFrame column name (required if input_data is DataFrame)
output_type (str): Output format - "dict" or "pydantic". Default: "dict"
max_retries (int): Maximum retry attempts on validation failure. Default: 3
model_name (Optional[str]): Model identifier
cache_dir (Optional[Path]): Custom cache directory
n_ctx (Optional[int]): Context window size
n_gpu_layers (Optional[int]): GPU layers
engine_kwargs (Any): Additional engine arguments
data.returns-1#Returns:
If input_data is str: Returns extracted data (dict or Pydantic model)
If input_data is DataFrame: Returns DataFrame with {target_col}_extracted column

data.choosing-a-model#Choosing a Model:
For Speed:
result = loclean.clean(df, target_col="data", model_name="tinyllama")
For Balance (Default):
result = loclean.clean(df, target_col="data")  # Uses phi-3-mini
For Quality:
result = loclean.extract(df, schema=ComplexSchema, model_name="qwen3-4b")
For Reasoning:
result = loclean.extract(df, schema=ReasoningSchema, model_name="deepseek-r1")
data.context-window#Context Window:
The n_ctx parameter controls the maximum context window size:
# Small context (faster, less memory)
result = loclean.clean(df, target_col="data", n_ctx=2048)
# Large context (slower, more memory, handles longer text)
result = loclean.extract(df, schema=Schema, n_ctx=8192)
Recommendations:
Simple extractions: 2048-4096
Complex schemas: 4096-8192
Very long text: 8192-16384
data.gpu-configuration#GPU Configuration:
Enable GPU acceleration:
# Use GPU for first 20 layers
result = loclean.clean(
    df,
    target_col="data",
    n_gpu_layers=20
)

CUDA-compatible GPU
llama-cpp-python compiled with CUDA support
Benefits:

data.cache-management#Cache Management:
from loclean.cache import LocleanCache
cache = LocleanCache(cache_dir=Path("/custom/cache"))
# Clear all cached results
cache.clear()
# Get cache statistics
stats = cache.get_stats()
data.custom-engine-instance#Custom Engine Ins:
For advanced use cases, create a custom engine:
from loclean.inference.local.llama_cpp import LlamaCppEngine
engine = LlamaCppEngine(
    model_name="qwen3-4b",
    cache_dir=Path("/custom/cache"),
    n_ctx=8192,
    n_gpu_layers=20
)
# Use with NarwhalsEngine directly
from loclean.engine.narwhals_ops import NarwhalsEngine
result = NarwhalsEngine.process_column(
    df,
    "data",
    engine,
    "Extract value"
)

from loclean.inference.factory import create_engine
from loclean.inference.config import EngineConfig
